author: @krap101
text:
Hello Robert,

I found your channel earlier this week and just wanted to start off by saying that this is truly one of those hidden Youtube gems and is a great resource. I did have a question however: Is the only purpose of the Fe2Cl3 and AlCl3 to modify the electrical properties by replacing the previously intercalated sulfates? I'm trying to make expanded graphite for a paraffin PCM heatsink and it seems like the best place to branch off is before the iron chloride is added and I can move to heating?

Thanks!!!

Danny

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate and i think you are spot on there - if you want expanded graphite the easiest method is bisulphate intercalation

replies.author: @krap101
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Great! Thanks!!!

replies.author: @andrewoneal306
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith if intercalating using bisulfate do you still use the same Ratio of sulfuric and phosphoric acids or is it a different method all together?

replies.author: @krap101
replies.text: Andrew ONeal Bisulfate refers to the number of sulfates per graphite particle. The sulfuric acid, phosphoric acid, potassium pernanganate is one method of bisulfate intercalation based on Hummer's method. Someone else can jump in if I got something wrong.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it's a different method mate - check out google scholar for guidance

author: @YangSword9x
text:
Hey Robert, I've been trying to learn how to make these graphene supercitors that the University of LA did on youtube, and I was wondering if I could give you money to make them for me, and have a video with all the simple steps(baby steps, yes hold my hand)  for me to learn how to  make them too.  Quantity I want is 2 things:

1) a graphene supercapacitor to replace a trucks 12 volt starter battery (same amount of energy replaced)
2) a graphene supercapacitor to replace laptops, flashlights, and my Toyota Prius 2004 2nd Generation 201.7 volts high voltage battery.

How much money do I need do you estimate?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Hiya mate - prototyping is very very expensive - your best bet is to make them yourself - there is a lot more to it than you seem to think - for a start it's not just a question of making them you have to test them for durability too and that takes time and money

Potassium permanganate
Iron chloride
DI water

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Unfortunately graphite is pretty tough stuff - at least chemically. You need pretty harsh conditions to get it to do anything. But, it's stability is also part of its appeal. The ampoule method uses no waste reagents but the energy cost is significantly higher - you have to cook the thing at a high temperature for 8 hours! I guess you pays your money you takes your choice! - if you find something though - let me know - and I am constantly on the lookout for greener approaches.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: There has been some reports that graphene oxide is conductive without reduction to graphene if you can clean out the oxidation rubbish ( they used NaOH) but you still need to disperse it first and herein lies the problem!

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Oxidation acts by forcing the plates apart and so weakening the van der waals forces enough that things like sonication will disperse it at a thin enough layer. Then of course you have to reduce it back to graphene. Just mixing the sludge wouldn't work because you have missed out the dispersion step and after reduction all you would get is graphite.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text:
Yes it would - but it would be non conductive. The steps are
oxidise graphite to graphite oxide
disperse oxide to make graphene oxide
reduce to  graphene
you have to do it this way because graphite and graphene are the same thing basically. The only difference is structural. Graphene is one atom thick. In order to strip it down you have to overcome the van der waals forces holding the graphene into the graphite form.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I am taking the top off right now and checking! - cheers mate.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: That's exactly what it is. The disperse the GO in water via sonication. The problem here for the home experimenter is that a sonicator costs about $4,000. I am trying to develop a method that anyone can do with a reasonably equipped  home workshop

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Oh, I should add - i am playing around with this to see if i can improve it and get better conductivity than the metal - that would be interesting

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: I'm not sure about that but it certainly gets close to the conductivity of what ever metal you choose to intercalate - so for aluminium chloride (if you used that) would conduct just below the level tah aluminium metal would.

author: @Shanjaq
text: Aye, it sounds like the pickup element crashed into its casing, which has a rigid constraint to the chassis, which results in a kind of "conduction microphonic" effect where the slightest motion in the area is picked up incredibly loud & clipping (kinda the same way that one can hear every motion in the house with one's head submerged in the tub)
